content: COVID-19 is caused by SARS-CoV-2, a betacoronavirus with spike protein mediating ACE2 receptor entry. Clinical spectrum ranges from asymptomatic infection to severe pneumonia with ARDS, multiorgan failure, and death. Long COVID (post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2) involves symptoms persisting ≥3 months after acute infection: fatigue, dyspnea, cognitive dysfunction ('brain fog'), post-exertional malaise, autonomic dysfunction (POTS). Pathophysiology includes viral persistence, immune dysregulation, endothelial dysfunction, and microclots. Variants of concern have evolved: Alpha, Delta, and predominantly Omicron lineages (BA.1, BA.2, BA.4/5, XBB, JN.1) with increased transmissibility and immune escape but generally reduced severity in vaccinated populations.

Outpatient treatment for high-risk patients within 5 days of symptom onset: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) preferred (89% reduction in hospitalization/death in EPIC-HR); remdesivir IV x3 days alternative; molnupiravir if others unavailable/contraindicated. Drug-drug interactions with Paxlovid require review (CYP3A substrate medications). Outpatient supportive care: antipyretics, hydration, monitoring for deterioration. Hospitalized patients not requiring oxygen: dexamethasone 6mg daily + remdesivir. Requiring oxygen: dexamethasone + remdesivir; consider tocilizumab or baricitinib for rapidly progressive disease. ICU patients: dexamethasone + tocilizumab/baricitinib; anticoagulation prophylaxis unless contraindicated. Monoclonal antibodies largely ineffective against current Omicron variants.

COVID-19 diagnosis: 1) NAAT/PCR (gold standard, highest sensitivity, detects viral RNA); 2) Rapid antigen tests (lower sensitivity, best for symptomatic patients within first week, serial testing improves sensitivity); 3) Antibody tests indicate prior infection/vaccination but not acute diagnosis. Testing indicated for symptomatic individuals, post-exposure screening, pre-procedure requirements. Isolation guidance: stay home until fever-free 24 hours without antipyretics and symptoms improving; wear mask through day 10. Severity classification: mild (no hypoxia), moderate (oxygen saturation ≥90% on room air), severe (<90% on room air, respiratory rate >30, lung infiltrates >50%), critical (respiratory failure, septic shock, organ dysfunction).
